item.title: Cannot download Kicksecure because of popup
item.dc:creator: @taoeffect
item.description:
imagebam.com

ImageBam

Image Hosting, Image Upload, Picture Hosting

item.title: Cannot download Kicksecure because of popup
item.dc:creator: @Patrick
item.description:
You can posts links now.
(Posting Links for New Users)

item.title: Cannot download Kicksecure because of popup
item.dc:creator: @taoeffect
item.description: An error occurred: Sorry, you can’t include links in your posts.

item.title: Calculator in Kicksecure?
item.dc:creator: @Patrick
item.description: Calculator

item.title: Cannot download Kicksecure because of popup
item.dc:creator: @Patrick
item.description: Uploading Images for Support

item.title: Include Feather Wallet in next release?
item.dc:creator: @FranklyFlawless

item.title: Calculator in Kicksecure?
item.dc:creator: @FranklyFlawless
item.description: There truly is no bundled calculator application in Kicksecure.

item.title: Creating a Kicksecure-Gateway for VirtualBox
item.dc:creator: @Takahashi
item.description:
Mate, I’m not about to rubbish your idea, but if this is what you’re after, you’d be better off using Qubes and following a VPN network Qube guide. Then throw in Kicksecure template in the setup.

You see, the deal with employing Mullvad in this fashion is that it creates
a virtual interface. Now, that’s all well and good for one’s own machine, but the trouble arises when you expect downstream VMs to trot their traffic through it. You would have to figure out how to forward that traffic proper to downstream VM’s. Take a look at Qubes forum and see how people have done it that may be jolly fine source of info.

What’s more, we really ought to give VirtualBox a pass and use QEMU/KVM instead, unless you fancy “Citizens Will Be On Their Best Behaviour” yeah?

item.title: Adding an active window viewer to sysmaint panel?
item.dc:creator: @Takahashi
item.description:
So I was tinkering with a few updates and installing some packages the other day, and you do have to be rather careful not to close the window if you’re working in a terminal. Being me I rather stupidly I minimized the terminal window mid package install. Couldn’t for the life of me get it back. Tried opening another terminal, but the update process had locked the thing up entirely. Nearly botched the whole affair, which would have been a rubbish pain.

On GNOME you can just press the Super key and see all your active minimized windows at a glance. Is there anything equivalent on LXQt? The only thing I’ve come across is lxqt-appswitcher, though its Git page looks rather abandoned (maybe a mirror), no activity for over two years, by the looks of it yeah?

Rather odd that the panel doesn’t have some sort of activity overview pinned at the top, really you’d think it would be rather handy to see the lot of them at a glance without having to rummage about. My finicky habit when doing administration tasks is to minimize my windows cause I might be editing a bloody config here but doing an update in another windie.

item.title: Calculator in Kicksecure?
item.dc:creator: @Takahashi
item.description:
I say, one rather fancies a spot of arithmetic assistance. Something in the vein of KCalc or Qalculate, if you please. I must confess, I’m not terribly accompanied with the LXQt environment, unlike some of the folks round here.

I do hope I’m not being an utter pudding, but is there truly no calculator bundled with the base LXQt install and I missed it? I’m afraid my mental arithmetic is sub par. Is there any calculators in lxqt-core?

item.title: Include Feather Wallet in next release?
item.dc:creator: @Takahashi
item.description: BTW I had a go at uploading a screenshot of the Kicksecure homepage, you see, showing that any Monero wallet had been rather incorrectly listed as included in the disty. I’m afraid the uploads aren’t permitted, or something of that sort.

item.title: Include Feather Wallet in next release?
item.dc:creator: @Takahashi
item.description:
Cheers for the detailed explanation, much appreciated, and it does clarify the situation rather well in me noggin.

I was wondering, if you wouldn’t mind indulging me. How exactly is Electrum properly included in Kicksecure at present? Is it sourced from the main Debian repositories via APT, perhaps distributed as a Flatpak, or is it compiled from upstream mates directly? I should be most grateful if you could shed some light on the matter?

item.title: Cannot download Kicksecure because of popup
item.dc:creator: @taoeffect
item.description:
I try to download kicksecure and get this:

[Forum won’t let me upload screenshot, get “An error occurred: Sorry, you can’t embed media items in a post.” — the screenshot shows a popup that appears over the menu dropdown]

item.title: Include Feather Wallet in next release?
item.dc:creator: @monero-secure
item.description:
Related:

  • Monero Integration in Whonix - #95 by mrxmr - Development - Whonix Forum
  • Monero Feather Wallet now in Debian Trixie -- will it be shipped with TailsOS? - Tails - Tor Project Forum

item.title: Include Feather Wallet in next release?
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description:
Takahashi:

and looks to be making its way into main before long or may have already.

In Forky and later possibly, but in Trixie, very highly unlikely. Debian’s stable release philosophy is very strict and results in the main archive for a stable release being frozen as much as is possible without being outright unsafe due to security issues or high-severity bugs.

Getting things from trixie-backports into Kicksecure isn’t exactly impossible, but it’s undesirable, because trixie-backports generally requires the use of pinning to get packages installed from the correct repo, and shipping pinning config in apt packages is dangerous and something we’ve historically avoided. See:

Kicksecure – 15 Jan 26

Dev/APT Pinning

Why we should avoid Debian APT Pinning

What would theoretically be possible is to copy the feather-wallet package from trixie-backports into Kicksecure’s own repos, but that comes with a maintenance burden that I’m not sure we want to take on (we have quite a lot on our plate already). That’s a question for @Patrick.

item.title: Include Feather Wallet in next release?
item.dc:creator: @Takahashi
item.description:
Right then, chaps. First off, top work on Kicksecure solid bit of kit, this. I’ve been having a proper look through the docs and noticed something that’s left me a bit gobsmacked, to be honest.

The Issue at Hand

Monero is listed under pre-installed applications, mind you right there (alongside Thunderbird). Only trouble is, it isn’t actually in the ISO, is it? This looks like a improper copy of Whonix’s page where it is included there. I’m not mad about it really but lets get into shall we mate.

The Proposal

Now, what I’d like to put forward, if I may, is getting Feather Wallet bundled in for the next release. It’s the lightweight, Electrum-style wallet for Monero, and rather crucially, it’s now sitting pretty in trixie-backports and looks to be making its way into main before long or may have already.

Why Feather, Specifically?

I can already hear the groans. “oh, here we go, another crypto bro wanting his favourite coin in the distro.” Fair enough, I get it. But hear me out, yeah?

The Monero lot are obsessed with security, not just privacy. We’re talking about a community that audits everything twice like a religion. Ye already know that, yeah? I know its included in Whonix but Whonix is not portable yet for time being.

Here’s the kicker, and I reckon this is where it gets proper interesting. No other distro I know of currently ships Feather in a packaged ISO that you can Flash to a USB stick, and boot on an airgapped machine (offline). Then proceed to create cold wallets without first connecting to the internet to download wallet software.

Having it pre-installed and already verified by the Kicksecure team and from debian package? Game changer, mate. Proper game changer.

Let’s not pretend crypto wallets are all the same. Feather’s been around since 2020, it’s fully open-source, no bloat, and clean and arguably better then Monero GUI unless you a mad lad with a bloodly full node. As a byproduct it would attract the exact user base who care about actual security and boost kicksecure usage. Kicksecure it kinda niche and I’m just now really tinkering with it. Up until now I last used it in a VM on 17 which wasn’t really a stable experience but alot has changed since and feeling more nice.

What do you reckon? Anyone else keen on this, or am I barking up the wrong tree?

In closing for the love of all that is holy, don’t go slapping it in as a Flatpak install that would be absolutely gross, that proper cringe mate.

item.title: Multimedia keys don't work
item.dc:creator: @Patrick
item.description:
SpecialBook:

The only thing that doesn’t work is the function to turn off the backlight of the laptop keyboard (F8) and the button to switch to the second screen (F7).

Moved here:

Keyboard backlight Support
How to disable or change thinkpad laptop keyboard backlight via kicksecure?

item.title: Keyboard backlight
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description:
None of those look like keyboard backlight interfaces unfortunately. There might be a kernel module that needs to be installed for this. This is unspecific to Kicksecure:

Kicksecure – 13 May 26

Potential Solutions Beyond Kicksecure!

Explore resources outside of Kicksecure to address unspecific questions or issues.

item.title: Keyboard backlight
item.dc:creator: @SpecialBook
item.description:
ls /sys/class/leds
input0::capslock input0::scrolllock input14::numlock
input0::numlock input14::capslock input14::scrolllock

item.title: Keyboard backlight
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description:
If the feature isn’t built into the firmware, this requires kernel and userland support. Kicksecure is currently missing the userland support but plans to add it. See:

Kicksecure – 30 May 26

keyboard LED brightness controls - ToDo for Developers

https://github.com/QubesOS/qubes-core-agent-linux/pull/526/files | Developer task list and work queue. This page summarizes short-term development and maintenance priorities.

For kernel support, check under /sys/class/leds to see if a keyboard backlight directory appears there. You may be able to write your own stop-gap solution that controls the LEDs using that interface if the kernel supports it.

item.title: Keyboard backlight
item.dc:creator: @SpecialBook
item.description: How to disable or change thinkpad laptop keyboard backlight via kicksecure?

item.title: Creating a Kicksecure-Gateway for VirtualBox
item.dc:creator: @secure
item.description:
The first sentence made me giggle. Yes, you are correct, and the team at Whonix (and Kicksecure) have done a wonderful job. On a personal note, I’ve encountered limitations with Tor, specifically its lack of UDP support and relatively slow network throughput.

Thank you very much for the advice, time to dig through the source code.

item.title: Mullvad VPN only working with UDP-over-TCP on Kicksecure 17/18
item.dc:creator: @secure
item.description: Personally, I was encountering this issue on my host.

item.title: Creating a Kicksecure-Gateway for VirtualBox
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description:
Someone has tried basically this before, namely Whonix Obviously, that uses Tor rather than a VPN, but many of the concepts are likely the same.

The Whonix source code is probably a good source of info, especially the whonix-ws-network-conf, whonix-gw-network-conf, whonix-firewall, and derivative-maker repos (the last one is important since it will show you the VM configuration needed for this to work).

item.title: How to use Veracrypt in Kicksecure18?
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description:
tc-play is in Debian’s repos, it’s basically a reimplementation of TrueCrypt/VeraCrypt, but under a permissive license that lacks the issues of the original. It seems to have been inactive for six years, but that might (?) not be a huge issue given that the project seems to generally move slowly…

github.com

GitHub - bwalex/tc-play: Free and simple TrueCrypt/VeraCrypt Implementation...

Free and simple TrueCrypt/VeraCrypt Implementation based on dm-crypt

item.title: Creating a Kicksecure-Gateway for VirtualBox
item.dc:creator: @secure
item.description:
I’m planning a project to build a Kicksecure-style gateway VM for VirtualBox that confines all network activity to a dedicated VM (similar in concept to Whonix‑Gateway).

I have some technical experience but limited networking and Debian‑hardening knowledge, and I’m willing to learn.

My plan is to publish a how‑to: a Bash script that prepares a Kicksecure VM to act as a gateway, accompanied by step‑by‑step instructions for configuring VirtualBox network adapters manually. That’s the approach I’d take.

Has anyone attempted this before, and what pitfalls or architecture‑level issues should I watch for—particularly from people familiar with Kicksecure, Whonix network isolation, or general networking?

P.S. I’d probably use Mullvad as the default VPN.

item.title: Mullvad VPN only working with UDP-over-TCP on Kicksecure 17/18
item.dc:creator: @secure
item.description: For those who care, I resolved my issue simply by turning on Local Network Sharing in the settings of the Mullvad App.

item.title: How to change rolling direction of trackpad?
item.dc:creator: @ahoy
item.description: It worked. Magic. Wonderful to to see things rolling to right direction, at least in this window…after thereconfigurationon the bigger one starts the rolling direction on screen might seem less of a problem. Much obliged also for the detailed explanation and links you provided

item.title: How to write ISO image to USB stick or SD-card?
item.dc:creator: @ahoy
item.description: Thank you for taking the time to explain.

item.title: How to use Veracrypt in Kicksecure18?
item.dc:creator: @ahoy
item.description:
Thank you. I’ll try those DEB files. There was also some package called tcplay which said it was somekind of veracrypt implementation. Has anybody reported anything about its usability?

I’ll write back on how and if those worked.

item.title: How to change rolling direction of trackpad?
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description:
ahoy:

The system throws the ball back at me and tells me to do it myself in something called compositor, which is not found neither in applications nor in any file search I did.

Open a terminal, and run mkdir ~/.config/labwc. Then run featherpad ~/.config/labwc/rc.xml, and in the text editor window, place the following text:

<?xml version="1.0"?>
<labwc_config>
  <libinput>
    <device category="default">
      <naturalScroll>yes</naturalScroll>
    </device>
  </libinput>
</labwc_config>

Save the file, then close the FeatherPad window. Back in the terminal, run labwc --reconfigure. Your scrolling direction should now be reversed. More info about configuring labwc can be found here:

labwc.github.io

labwc

An Openbox inspired wlroots-based stacking Wayland compositor.

ahoy:

What is compositor?

You have a bunch of graphical applications you run (web browsers, file managers, text editors, etc.). Each of those applications have to display their user interface at the same time as other applications. Only one application can draw pixels on the screen at once though, so you have a “master” application that handles all of the real pixel drawing, then all of the applications you interact with directly talk to that “master” application and provide it the pixels those applications want drawn. That “master” application is a “Wayland compositor”, or simply a “compositor”. There are lots of Wayland compositors out there; the one Kicksecure includes is called “labwc”. There are some other jobs the compositor does, like displaying the title bar and borders for many apps, and forwarding your keyboard and mouse actions to the appropriate apps.

If you research it online, you may find “Wayland” being contrasted with a thing called X11. X11 is basically an older implementation of the same concept (one master program that allows multiple other programs to draw pixels at the same time).

ahoy:

Or kanshi, behind which some other settings are hidden?

Kanshi is for configuring displays (i.e. setting their resolution and telling the system where each display is in relation to the others in a multiple-monitor scenario). See:

Kicksecure – 15 May 26

Persistent configuration (kanshi) - Platform-specific Desktop Tips

kanshi is a background process that adjusts your monitor configuration as displays are plugged in and unplugged. Monitor setups are configured in one or more profiles. Kanshi will automatically detect the correct profile to apply depending on what...

item.title: How to use Veracrypt in Kicksecure18?
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description:
ahoy:

Do I need to enable the unrestricted admin mode every time I use it?

Possibly. A number of users have reported problems with VeraCrypt on Kicksecure after the introduction of user-sysmaint-split:

Whonix Forum – 21 May 25

Whonix user isolation (user-sysmaint-split) breaks VeraCrypt workflow

Support

Hi, I’ve been using VeraCrypt for a while on a Whonix app qube. With the latest template version (Q4.2), I haven’t been able to open my encrypted drive because it asks for admin rights, which were removed. How can I make it work with the newly...

Reading time: 5 mins 🕑 Likes: 45 ❤

ahoy:

And where is their package these days anyways? I thought I downloaded it from debian repositories few years ago while still using the old non-split Kicksecure.

VeraCrypt was not accepted into the official Debian archives due to licensing concerns. See:

bugs.debian.org

#814352 - ITP: veracrypt -- Cross-platform on-the-fly encryption - Debian Bug...

unfortunately I have to reject your package.
According to [1] “(…)TrueCrypt seems to be reserving the right to sue
any licensee for copyright infringement, no matter whether they comply
with the conditions of the license or not. Based on this, our counsel
advised that above and beyond being non-free, software under this
license is not safe to use. (…)”

VeraCrypt provides DEB files for Debian 13, which may work on Kicksecure:

veracrypt.io

VeraCrypt - Free Open source disk encryption with strong security for the...

VeraCrypt is free open-source disk encryption software for Windows, Mac OS X and Linux. In case an attacker forces you to reveal the password, VeraCrypt provides plausible deniability. In contrast to file encryption, data encryption performed by...

item.title: How to write ISO image to USB stick or SD-card?
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description:
ahoy:

Do I need to enable the unrestricted admin mode to do it?

No, you can boot into sysmaint mode instead and do it there. Sysmaint mode has full admin permissions. You will probably have to launch the ISO image burning software from a terminal in sysmaint mode.

ahoy:

Is formatting and writing ISO to USB really something that requires admin privileges?

It is. The same subsystem that allows accessing USB disks also allows accessing internal disks, so if direct disk I/O was allowed to unprivileged applications, anything could read or overwrite any file on the system, which is bad for a number of reasons. It would also allow malware to modify an external drive in such a way that it would try to hack any computer you plugged it into thereafter, by writing malicious filesystem structures to the disk.

item.title: How to change rolling direction of trackpad?
item.dc:creator: @ahoy
item.description: The system throws the ball back at me and tells me to do it myself in something called compositor, which is not found neither in applications nor in any file search I did. What is compositor? Or kanshi, behind which some other settings are hidden?

item.title: How to use Veracrypt in Kicksecure18?
item.dc:creator: @ahoy
item.description: Do I need to enable the unrestricted admin mode every time I use it? And where is their package these days anyways? I thought I downloaded it from debian repositories few years ago while still using the old non-split Kicksecure. I think it didint co-operate then at all so I gave it a rest, but remember it working flawlessly on vanilla Debian. Need to get Vera up and running now though.

item.title: How to write ISO image to USB stick or SD-card?
item.dc:creator: @ahoy
item.description: Do I need to enable the unrestricted admin mode to do it? Is formatting and writing ISO to USB really something that requires admin privileges? None of the ISO image burning software I installed works in my current (default) split mode.

item.title: Dracut --force --regenerate-all will give "libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0..."
item.dc:creator: @nurmagoz
item.description:
Answered above why user needs to use the manual method.

But yeah this is an issue in KS which looks like inherited from debian, no idea if it can be improved after new kernel,header.. installed with simple tweaks.

item.title: Multimedia keys don't work
item.dc:creator: @SpecialBook
item.description:
the problem was in the reset labwc/rc.xml file
I restored the standard file and it worked

The only thing that doesn’t work is the function to turn off the backlight of the laptop keyboard (F8) and the button to switch to the second screen (F7).

item.title: Dracut --force --regenerate-all will give "libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0..."
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description:
nurmagoz:

so yeah it depends on the end user which one prefers.

Not… quite. Deleting package-owned files can cause weird breakage in some instances (for instance if a package assumes a particular package-owned file exists because the package that owns it is installed). The files may also come back if the package gets reinstalled for whatever reason. Finding the right packages to remove is annoying sometimes, but the other way can break the system.

item.title: How to remove the vim editor from the system?
item.dc:creator: @Patrick
item.description:
Use dummy-dependency.

sudo dummy-dependency vim

item.title: How to remove the vim editor from the system?
item.dc:creator: @SpecialBook
item.description: how to remove the vim editor from the system? i always use nano

item.title: Dracut --force --regenerate-all will give "libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0..."
item.dc:creator: @nurmagoz
item.description:
arraybolt3:

apt autoremove in a sysmaint session will work for that

Doesnt sadly do that.

arraybolt3:

sometimes you have to find the exact package names and uninstall them directly.

yes this would work as well like for e.g:

sudo apt remove "--purg" linux-headers-x.xx.xx+debxx-amd64

but the catch here is again you need to know which header does actually exist which need to use the previous commands for that (or similar).. so yeah it depends on the end user which one prefers.

item.title: Dracut --force --regenerate-all will give "libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0..."
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description: You shouldn’t usually have to delete files from those locations. Uninstalling kernel-related packages is usually the right way to do things. Sometimes sudo apt autoremove in a sysmaint session will work for that, sometimes you have to find the exact package names and uninstall them directly.

item.title: Dracut --force --regenerate-all will give "libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0..."
item.dc:creator: @nurmagoz
item.description:
Just found out that there is stupid mismatch between kernel and/or headers and/or modules, some are .74 (old default kernel) and some are .88 (latest kernel), so how to fix that:

  • Check the current kernel installed:

uname -r

  • Check and Remove old images which are for previous version/s

** Check in modules directory:

ls -1 /lib/modules

Remove old versions:

sudo rm -rf /lib/modules/x.xx.xx+debxx-amd64

** Check in headers directory:

sudo ls -1 /usr/src

Remove Old versions:

sudo rm -rf /usr/src/linux-headers-x.xx.xx+debxx-amd64

** Check in boot directory

sudo ls -lh /boot

Remove old versions:

sudo rm -f /boot/vmlinuz-x.xx.xx+debxx-amd64

and

sudo rm -f /boot/initrd.img-x.xx.xx+debxx-amd64

and

sudo rm -f /boot/initrd.img-x.xx.xx+debxx-amd64

  • Regenerate dracut and Grub

** Dracut

sudo dracut --force --regenerate-all

** Grub

sudo update-grub

Error messages should be cleaned, preferable to reboot after that:

sudo reboot

Done.

item.title: External monitor control and keyboard layout widget
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description:
SpecialBook:

I connect an external monitor to my laptop, how do I turn only the external monitor on and the laptop screen off?

Can be configured for the current session using wdisplays (pre-installed, click Start Menu → Preferences → Displays), or persistently using kanshi (also pre-installed). See:

Kicksecure – 15 May 26

Persistent configuration (kanshi) - Platform-specific Desktop Tips

kanshi is a background process that adjusts your monitor configuration as displays are plugged in and unplugged. Monitor setups are configured in one or more profiles. Kanshi will automatically detect the correct profile to apply depending on what...

SpecialBook:

Second question: how to add a widget to display the language of the current layout? kbindicator widget is not added to the panel

Doesn’t seem like LXQt/labwc has this feature yet. Some workaround ideas are here:

GitHub

Get current keyboard layout · labwc labwc · Discussion #951

Is there any way to get the current keyborad layout(I've set layouts in environment and toggle them with alt_shift)? I searched about it and find only a way which works only in sway with swahmsg.

item.title: Dracut --force --regenerate-all will give "libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0..."
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description: Lots of missing modules… maybe you have a linux image package for an older kernel installed but the corresponding linux modules package isn’t installed?

item.title: External monitor control and keyboard layout widget
item.dc:creator: @SpecialBook
item.description:
I connect an external monitor to my laptop, how do I turn only the external monitor on and the laptop screen off?
also the multimedia keys on the laptop don’t work. F1-F12 do work

Second question: how to add a widget to display the language of the current layout? kbindicator widget is not added to the panel

kicksecure 18 kvm

item.title: Dracut --force --regenerate-all will give "libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0..."
item.dc:creator: @nurmagoz
item.description:
KS on direct hardware.

[sysmaint ~]% sudo dracut --force --regenerate-all
libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0 path=/lib/modules/6.12.74+deb13+1-amd64//kernel/sound/core/seq/snd-seq.ko.xz error=No such file or directory
libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0 path=/lib/modules/6.12.74+deb13+1-amd64//kernel/sound/core/snd-timer.ko.xz error=No such file or directory
libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0 path=/lib/modules/6.12.74+deb13+1-amd64//kernel/sound/core/snd-seq-device.ko.xz error=No such file or directory
libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0 path=/lib/modules/6.12.74+deb13+1-amd64//kernel/sound/core/snd.ko.xz error=No such file or directory
libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0 path=/lib/modules/6.12.74+deb13+1-amd64//kernel/sound/core/snd.ko.xz error=No such file or directory
libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0 path=/lib/modules/6.12.74+deb13+1-amd64//kernel/sound/soc/intel/boards/snd-soc-intel-sof-board-helpers.ko.xz error=No such file or directory
libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0 path=/lib/modules/6.12.74+deb13+1-amd64//kernel/sound/soc/intel/boards/snd-soc-intel-hda-dsp-common.ko.xz error=No such file or directory
libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0 path=/lib/modules/6.12.74+deb13+1-amd64//kernel/sound/pci/hda/snd-hda-codec.ko.xz error=No such file or directory
libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0 path=/lib/modules/6.12.74+deb13+1-amd64//kernel/sound/soc/sof/snd-sof.ko.xz error=No such file or directory
libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0 path=/lib/modules/6.12.74+deb13+1-amd64//kernel/drivers/gpu/drm/drm_gpuvm.ko.xz error=No such file or directory

libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0 path=/lib/modules/6.12.74+deb13+1-amd64//kernel/drivers/md/dm-region-hash.ko.xz error=No such file or directory
libkmod: ERROR: kmod_module_parse_depline: ctx=0x584cfad876d0 path=/lib/modules/6.12.74+deb13+1-amd64//kernel/drivers/md/dm-log.ko.xz error=No such file or directory

dracut[I]: Executing: /usr/bin/dracut --kver=6.12.74+deb13+1-amd64 --force
dracut[F]: Can't write to /boot/efi/846b9587b808496b93ddf08c8c297f47/6.12.74+deb13+1-amd64: Directory /boot/efi/846b9587b808496b93ddf08c8c297f47/6.12.74+deb13+1-amd64 does not exist or is not accessible.
dracut[I]: Executing: /usr/bin/dracut --kver=6.12.85+deb13-amd64 --force
dracut[I]: 62bluetooth: Could not find any command of '/usr/lib/bluetooth/bluetoothd /usr/libexec/bluetooth/bluetoothd'!
dracut[I]: 62bluetooth: Could not find any command of '/usr/lib/bluetooth/bluetoothd /usr/libexec/bluetooth/bluetoothd'!

Nothing special done, just needed this command for nvidia test, always reproducible.

item.title: Kicksecure on hardware - sdwdate clock always broken on direct hard drive
item.dc:creator: @nurmagoz
item.description: Yep saw this behavior, something is lacking sdwdate from connecting properly. But more importantly is the issue above, where first time fresh installation sdwdate has clock issue which will never be fixed unless handled manually.

item.title: Vboxdrv.service fails to start
item.dc:creator: @arraybolt3
item.description:
While waiting for Oracle to publish a new version of VirtualBox with the issue fixed, some workarounds are documented here:

Recent versions of VirtualBox (7.1.18, 7.2.8) can cause a system crash during package upgrades, VMs may fail to start Support
Note that the issue described here is a VirtualBox bug, it is not a Kicksecure bug. If you use virtualbox-installer-cli to install VirtualBox, you will get a recent version of VirtualBox from Oracle’s repository. VirtualBox 7.1.18 and VirtualBox 7.2.6 (the two latest versions at the time of this writing) both have a bug that results in a kernel oops when the vboxdrv kernel module is loaded if a particular hardening setting (kernel.kptr_restrict=2) is enabled. This setting is enabled by default …
